questions.question: What did MAST find about multi-agent failure rates, and what are its three root categories?
options: Failures under 5%; categorized by language
41-86.7% failure rates across 7 open-source MAS (1,642 traces) — rooted in Specification Problems (41.77%), Coordination Failures (36.94%), and Verification Gaps (21.30%)
Failures only in systems over 100 agents
All failures trace to GPU errors
questions.explanation: Cemri et al.'s NeurIPS 2025 taxonomy turned arbitrary-looking failure numbers into structure. 2026 production practice: your architecture isn't done until you can name the deployed mitigation for each MAST category.

questions.question: Why are Specification Problems the largest failure category?
options: Models are too small to parse specs
Role ambiguity (two agents both think they're the reviewer), underspecified tasks, and implicit success criteria — the agent literally cannot tell if it succeeded
Specs are written in legalese
Specification tooling is missing from frameworks
questions.explanation: Mitigations are organizational, not model-level: explicit role contracts stating what each agent does AND doesn't do, per-task acceptance tests defining "done," and a pre-flight spec check before dispatch.

questions.question: What is a retry storm, and what stops it?
options: A user clicking retry repeatedly; a debounce timer
A payment failure triggers order retries, which trigger inventory retries, overwhelming the service with 10x load in seconds — circuit breakers stop the cascade
Network packet retransmission; TCP tuning
Agents re-asking the same question; deduplication
questions.explanation: Cascading reliability failure is the multi-agent version of a classic distributed-systems pathology: each layer's reasonable local retry policy multiplies into a system-wide flood. The breaker trips the offending path before the cascade compounds.

questions.question: Which failures make up the Groupthink family?
options: Slow consensus, fast consensus, no consensus

Monoculture collapse (shared base model → shared hallucinations), conformity bias, deficient theory of mind, mixed-motive drift to a compromise that satisfies no one, and cascading reliability failures
Overfitting, underfitting, and drift
Memory leaks in five flavors
questions.explanation: All five arise when agents homogenize or mimic each other. The countermeasures thread back through the phase: heterogeneous ensembles, adversarial debate roles, ToM prompting, and independent verification.

questions.question: How did STRATUS improve mitigation success?
options: By doubling the model size
Specialized detection, diagnosis, and validation agents — treating failure handling itself as a multi-agent role decomposition, reporting 1.5x mitigation-success improvement
By disabling retries entirely
By switching all agents to temperature 0
questions.explanation: The fix for multi-agent failure is, fittingly, specialized agents for the failure pipeline — the role-specialization insight applied to reliability engineering.
